metric_name: About as good as it gets
metric_value: For a new chip, this is about as good as it gets. The performance improvements are so evident that only a few minutes in the store demo area will convince many buyers that it's time to upgrade.
The G5 is a hot box and one which for the first time in several years gives Mac owners some bragging rights versus their industry-standard brethren.
The G5 processor, after months and years of speculation and anticipation, finally delivers a better, faster, cheaper, smaller solution for Apple.

metric_name: Power Mac G5 configs and pricing
metric_value: Apple Power Mac G5 models: 1.6 GHz (256 MB 333 MHz DDR, $2,000), 1.8 GHz (512 MB 400 MHz DDR, $2,400), dual 2.0 GHz ($3,000 later this month). First PC with 64-bit processing and 1 GHz front-side bus; 6.4 GB/s memory bandwidth; 16 GB/s in dual. 100,000+ pre-orders since Jun 23 introduction.

IDC's Alan Promisel: 'While the G5 is aimed at Apple's core audience — high-end users, gamers and audio-visual producers — the 64-bit desktop computer is being closely watched. It's definitely an architecture the commercial IT managers are keeping an eye on.'

Graphics designers and image manipulators will see vast improvements in their ability to apply filters because the code has been optimized for 64-bit computation. Customers in search of simulation as close to reality as possible will be willing to pay the G5's higher prices.

IBM failed to deliver a 3 GHz PowerPC G5 within 12 months as promised at the Jun 2003 introduction; G5 topped out at 2.7 GHz desktop (2005) and never shipped in laptop form due to heat/power — directly prompting Steve Jobs' WWDC Jun 2005 Intel-transition announcement.

Apple completed PowerPC-to-Intel transition by Aug 2006 (32 months after this article). Mac Pro with Intel Xeon replaced Power Mac G5 Aug 2006; partial validation of Kastner's 2003 enthusiasm (G5 did deliver the 2003 performance bump) but his 'bragging rights for the first time in several years' proved fleeting.
